MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Aug-15-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. Russell Higgins, Esq. of Knight Law Group, LLP (tel: 310-552-2250) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. Theodore Dorenkamp III, Esq. of Bowman And Brooke LLP (tel: 310-768-3068) for the Defendant.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT AMERICAN HONDA MOTOR CO., INC.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant American Honda Motor Co., Inc.'s motion for summary adjudication on the second cause of action for concealment is granted.

Defendant maintained its summary adjudication burden of production per CCP 437c(p)(2). Plaintiff's factually devoid discovery responses demonstrate that plaintiff has no proof of damage. (Lu Decl., Ex. 6 [Plaintiff's special interrogatory responses 13, 29, 35].) In opposition, plaintiff failed to file a complete opposition and merely proffered a continuance declaration under CCP 437c(h). The court denies the continuance request. Plaintiff has not been diligent. (Cooksey v. Alexakis (2004) 123 Cal.App.4th 246, 257 [lack of diligence basis to deny CCP 437c(h) continuance request].) This case has been pending for over two years, the trial date is next month, and she does not sufficiently explain why this discovery did not occur earlier. Moreover, the discovery that plaintiff seeks (depose defendant's employees) does not relate to plaintiff's damages. Plaintiff ought to have been able to produce damages evidence without the adduced discovery.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling.

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-11-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: DEFENDANT AMERICAN HONDA MOTOR CO., INC.'s DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant's demurrer to the second cause of action for concealment is overruled. Plaintiff alleges a sufficient relationship between plaintiff and defendant for the concealment claim. (Dhital v. Nissan N. Am. Inc. (2022) 84 Cal.App.5th 828, 844 [allegations that plaintiff purchased vehicle from Nissan dealership, that Nissan issued a warranty, and dealership was agent of Nissan were sufficient to show relationship with manufacturer for concealment claim] (review granted); CRC 8.1115(e)(1) [opinion may be cited for persuasive value where review granted].)

Plaintiff's active concealment and exclusive knowledge allegations are sufficient to support the concealment cause of action. (Heliotis v. Schuman (1986) 181 Cal.App.3d 646, 651 [party may plead concealment claim based on active concealment or exclusive knowledge where no fiduciary relationship exists]; Complaint, pars. 23-26, 28, 35, 37-43, 67, 69, 71, 74-75, 84-87, 111-122.) In Falk v. Gen. Motors Corp. (N.D. Cal. 2007) 496 F.Supp.2d 1088, 1096-97, the court found a duty to disclose based on "exclusive knowledge" where the manufacturer had superior knowledge of a defect that was not readily apparent. The court noted that customers are not required to conduct an internet search prior to purchasing a car. (Id. at 1097.)

In any event, plaintiff also alleges active concealment. (Complaint, par. 71.) The court rejects defendant's argument that the concealment claim is not alleged with the requisite particularity. Unlike an intentional misrepresentation claim, a plaintiff alleging a concealment claim likely has less information and the specificity requirement is "relaxed when it is apparent from the allegations that the defendant necessarily possesses knowledge of the facts." (Quelimane Co. v. Steward Title Guaranty Co. (1998) 19 Cal.4th 26, 47; see also Alfaro v. Community Housing Imp. System & Planning Ass'n, Inc. (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 1356, 1384.)

The court rejects defendant's argument that the economic loss rule bars the concealment claim. (Dhital, 84 Cal.App.5th at 840 ["we conclude plaintiffs' claim for fraudulent inducement by concealment is not subject to demurrer on the ground it is barred by the economic loss rule."]; Vega v. Jones, Day, Reavis & Pogue (2004) 121 Cal.App.4th 282, 291 ["Active concealment or suppression of facts by a nonfiduciary 'is the equivalent of a false representation, i.e., actual fraud.'
[Citation.]"].)

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/RBU)
